---
title: "American Coinwort (Centella erecta)"
slug: "centella-erecta"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622940473/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "American Coinwort"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Apiales"
tax_family: "Apiaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Apiales", "Apiaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/622940473/medium.jpg" alt="American Coinwort" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/sebento">
sebento</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/342113315">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Apiales
-   **Family:** Apiaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=160295)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in moist to wet habitats.

## Natural history notes

A low-growing herb characterized by its rounded, coin-shaped leaves on slender stems and small, inconspicuous flowers. In Florida, it is commonly found in moist to wet habitats such as lawns, ditches, and wet flatwoods. It grows during the warmer months and often spreads along the ground, forming patches in suitable conditions.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/160295-Centella-erecta)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/specimen/plantdetails/709)
